regulator: MAS (Monetary Authority of Singapore) -- sole AML/CFT regulator and supervisor for digital-asset-related business in Singapore; no separate crypto AML authority exists. Suspicious transaction reports filed by MAS-supervised digital payment token (DPT) service providers and digital token service providers (DTSPs) go to the Suspicious Transaction Reporting Office (STRO), a unit of the Commercial Affairs Department (CAD) of the Singapore Police Force -- per secondary description of MAS Notice PSN02's own 'STRO' definition, a receiving/investigative body rather than an independent digital-asset AML regulator or rule-maker; this research could not independently fetch PSN02's own text to confirm that definition verbatim this session (see open_items)
instruments: Payment Services Act 2019 (Act 2 of 2019, 'PS Act') -- the base licensing statute for digital payment token (DPT) services, widely and consistently reported (including by MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release, fetched below) as commenced 28 January 2020; this research could not independently fetch Singapore Statutes Online's consolidated PS Act text this session (sso.agc.gov.sg returned an HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried, including robots.txt, live-verified today) so the exact commencement date rests on secondary/MAS-footnote corroboration only, consistent with this jurisdiction's other pillar cards

Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, Bill No. 41/2020 (read first time 2 November 2020) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF from parliament.gov.sg; clause 1 states the Bill becomes the 'Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021' on commencement by Gazette notification; the Bill's own Explanatory Statement states verbatim, as reason (a) for the Bill: 'to widen the definition of "digital payment token service" in order to regulate activities of virtual asset service providers required to be regulated under new standards adopted by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) on anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism', and as reason (b): 'to widen the definition of "cross-border money transfer service" ... in order to deal with money laundering, terrorism financing, and reputational risks to Singapore'; clause 7 amends the PS Act's First Schedule, Part 3 to add token-transmission-facilitation, token-safeguarding (custody) and cross-border transfer-arranging services to the 'digital payment token service' definition -- the FATF-alignment mechanism named in the Explanatory Statement

MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg), since every path tried against www.mas.gov.sg this session (including this exact document's own -/media/ PDF URL) returned an identical 853,817-byte HTML page titled 'Maintenance', live-verified today; states that the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 and its subsidiary legislation take effect in stages from 4 April 2024, and that affected DPT service providers must, among other transitional deadlines measured from that date, submit a 'report on their compliance with AML/CFT requirements' within 9 months -- the direct textual link, in a genuinely fetched primary source, between the Amendment Act's 4 April 2024 commencement and an AML/CFT compliance-attestation obligation specific to DPT service providers

MAS Notice PSN02, 'Prevention of Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism -- Digital Payment Token Service' -- the operative AML/CFT rulebook for DPT service providers (risk assessment, customer due diligence, the crypto travel rule for transfers, correspondent-account/wire-transfer controls, record-keeping, suspicious-transaction reporting to STRO, and internal AML/CFT policies, compliance, audit and training functions); this research located, via search-engine-indexed document titles rather than independent direct fetch, a lineage of dated/revised versions -- an original notice dated 5 December 2019 (aligned with the PS Act's own 28 January 2020 commencement), followed by revisions dated 28 June 2021, 1 March 2022, then a reissue dated 2 April 2024 (effective from 4 April 2024, per the media release above), and a further revision dated 30 June 2025 (reported as effective 1 July 2025); every direct-fetch attempt against www.mas.gov.sg's own PSN02 PDF URLs (including the 2 April 2024 and 30 June 2025 versions) returned the same 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page this session, so this lineage is stated from indexed titles and secondary compliance-industry summaries only, not from independently read notice text -- see open_items

Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 (FSM Act), Part 9 -- the Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) licensing regime, commenced 30 June 2025, requiring a licence for token-related dealing/custody/exchange-facilitation services performed from Singapore solely for customers located outside Singapore; confirmed by direct fetch of MAS's own media release, 'MAS Clarifies Regulatory Regime for Digital Token Service Providers', 6 June 2025 (via sgpc.gov.sg, since www.mas.gov.sg blocks this document identically to PSN02 above), which states verbatim: 'The money laundering risks are higher in such business models and if their substantive regulated activity is outside of Singapore, MAS is unable to effectively supervise such persons' as MAS's own stated reason for setting 'the bar high for licensing' and stating it 'will generally not issue a licence' for that category; the release's own Annex cites FSM Act Section 136(1) for the 'digital token'/'digital token service'/'digital token service provider' definitions and Section 137 for the licensing requirement, and directs affected parties to an AMLCFT@mas.gov.sg mailbox -- itself further primary-source evidence that MAS frames this specific licensing gate as an AML/CFT-driven measure, not merely a market-conduct one

MAS AML/CFT Notice for Digital Token Service Licence holders (reported in secondary trade sources as 'MAS Notice FSM-N27', 'Prevention of Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism -- Holders of Digital Token Service Licence'), together with the Financial Services and Markets (Digital Token Service Providers) Regulations 2025 -- both reported as coming into operation 30 June 2025 alongside the FSM Act Part 9 regime itself, requiring DTSP licensees to perform customer due diligence on all existing customers, maintain an independent AML/CFT audit function, and report discovered suspicious activity or fraud incidents within 5 working days; this research could not independently fetch either instrument's text this session (both are hosted only on the blocked www.mas.gov.sg domain) so their content is stated from secondary legal-commentary summaries only -- see open_items

MAS Consultation Paper on Proposed Regulatory Approach, Regulations, Notices and Guidelines for Digital Token Service Providers under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 (4 October 2024) and MAS's Response to Feedback Received (30 May 2025) -- per MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release (fetched above), these are the two publications, alongside a first response to public consultation on 14 February 2022, through which MAS states it has 'consistently communicated' the DTSP regime's AML-risk-driven policy position 'for a few years'; this research did not independently fetch either underlying consultation-paper or feedback-response document this session (both link only to the blocked www.mas.gov.sg domain), and states their existence and dates only as recited inside the genuinely fetched 6 June 2025 release -- see open_items
standing_summary: Singapore's AML/CFT regime for digital-asset business is administered entirely by MAS -- simultaneously central bank and integrated financial regulator -- through two layers that this research was able to verify from genuinely fetched primary sources, plus a third layer (the core AML/CFT notice itself) that this research could describe only from secondary corroboration after every direct-fetch attempt against www.mas.gov.sg was blocked. First, on the FATF-alignment mechanism specifically flagged for verification: this research directly fetched the Payment Services (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg), which became the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 on commencement, and confirmed its own Explanatory Statement states, in MAS's/the Government's own drafting language, that the Bill's first purpose is 'to widen the definition of "digital payment token service" in order to regulate activities of virtual asset service providers required to be regulated under new standards adopted by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) on anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism', and its second purpose is to widen the 'cross-border money transfer service' definition 'in order to deal with money laundering, terrorism financing, and reputational risks to Singapore'. The Bill's clause 7 is the operative mechanism: it amends the PS Act's First Schedule, Part 3 definition of 'digital payment token service' to add token-transmission-facilitation, token/token-instrument-safeguarding (custody), and inducement-of-DPT-trading services -- precisely the broader activity set FATF's revised Recommendation 15 treats as within virtual-asset-service-provider scope. Although passed as the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021, this research found (via secondary corroboration, since Singapore Statutes Online returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried today, including robots.txt) that the Act did not actually commence until 4 April 2024, per a 'Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 (Commencement) Notification 2024' gazetted around 28 March-2 April 2024 -- consistent with, and confirmed independently by, MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release (fetched as a genuine PDF via the Singapore Government's press-release repository, sgpc.gov.sg, since mas.gov.sg itself serves an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML page on every path tried this session, including that document's own URL), which sets a transitional deadline requiring affected DPT service providers to submit an 'AML/CFT' compliance report within 9 months of that commencement date. Second, on the newer FSM Act Part 9 Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime: this research directly fetched MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release (again via sgpc.gov.sg) and confirmed it states, in MAS's own words, that from the regime's 30 June 2025 commencement, DTSPs serving customers solely outside Singapore require a licence that MAS 'will generally not issue', because 'the money laundering risks are higher in such business models and if their substantive regulated activity is outside of Singapore, MAS is unable to effectively supervise such persons' -- an explicit, primary-sourced statement that this licensing gate is an AML/CFT risk-control measure, reinforced by the release's own direction to an AMLCFT@mas.gov.sg contact mailbox and its Annex citation of FSM Act Section 136(1) (definitions) and Section 137 (licensing) as the operative provisions. The release also confirms DTSPs already serving Singapore customers 'are already subject to regulation' with 'no change to what the licensed providers can do', and that MAS's DTSP policy position has been 'consistently communicated ... for a few years' across a first consultation response (14 February 2022), a further consultation paper (4 October 2024) and a feedback response (30 May 2025) -- none of which this research independently fetched this session, since all are hosted only on the blocked www.mas.gov.sg domain (see open_items). Third, and this is where this research's sourcing discipline requires an honest gap: MAS Notice PSN02, the actual operative AML/CFT rulebook that sets DPT service providers' risk-assessment, customer-due-diligence, travel-rule, record-keeping and suspicious-transaction-reporting obligations (reporting to the Suspicious Transaction Reporting Office, STRO, a unit of the Commercial Affairs Department of the Singapore Police Force, per PSN02's own definition as described in secondary compliance-industry sources), could not itself be directly fetched this session -- every attempt against its www.mas.gov.sg PDF URLs, across multiple dated versions, returned the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page that blocks this domain uniformly. This research located, via search-engine-indexed document titles rather than independent reading, a version lineage -- an original PSN02 dated 5 December 2019 (aligned with the PS Act's own 28 January 2020 commencement), revisions dated 28 June 2021 and 1 March 2022, a reissue dated 2 April 2024 (effective 4 April 2024, corroborated by the genuinely fetched media release above), and a further revision dated 30 June 2025 (reported effective 1 July 2025, timed to the FSM Act Part 9 commencement) -- broadly matching the lineage the director's brief anticipated, but this card states it as indexed-title-sourced background, not as independently verified notice text, and it should be re-fetched and quoted directly once mas.gov.sg is reachable. On enforcement, this research specifically investigated, and can report an honest correction to, the one case the director's brief flagged: MAS's 30 June 2022 reprimand of Three Arrows Capital Pte. Ltd. for 'providing false information' and exceeding its assets-under-management threshold was found, on inspection of its own subject matter (via search-result summaries of MAS's own enforcement-action page, itself blocked to direct fetch this session), to be a registered-fund-management-company (RFMC) registration and disclosure breach -- false/misleading information about shareholding and directorship changes, and breach of an AUM ceiling -- not an AML/CFT-specific enforcement action; this card accordingly does not cite it as an AML/CFT enforcement precedent, correcting rather than confirming that element of the brief. This research separately searched for, but did not find, any MAS enforcement action specifically grounded in PSN02 or in AML/CFT breaches by a named DPT service provider or DTSP licensee: the two AML/CFT-related MAS enforcement actions this research did find dated 2025 -- composition penalties totalling S$960,000 against five major payment institutions (27 June 2025, for cross-border money transfer service breaches of the separate Notice PSN01, not PSN02) and composition penalties/prohibition orders totalling S$27.45 million against nine financial institutions and several individuals (July 2025, tied to the August 2023 S$3 billion money-laundering case) -- are both PSN01/traditional-financial-institution matters, not digital-payment-token or digital-token-service-provider AML/CFT enforcement, and so are not asserted in this card as digital-asset-specific precedent. As of this card's generation date, this research found no genuinely primary-sourced example of MAS taking AML/CFT enforcement action specifically against a named DPT service provider or DTSP licensee. On the regulator-structure negative the brief asked to be confirmed: this research's finding, consistent with this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar card, is that the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) has no independent digital-asset AML/CFT role (ordinary company registration only) and that the Singapore Police Force's Commercial Affairs Department functions, via its STRO unit, purely as the receiving and investigative body for suspicious transaction reports that MAS's own notices require DPT/DTSP licensees to file -- not as an independent digital-asset AML rule-maker or regulator; MAS remains the sole AML/CFT regulator for this sector.

key_links.label: Parliament of Singapore: Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, Bill No. 41/2020 (read first time 2 November 2020) -- official PDF; source of the FATF-alignment Explanatory Statement quotes above; fetch-verified live this session

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the 4 April 2024 commencement and 9-month AML/CFT attestation deadline

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Clarifies Regulatory Regime for Digital Token Service Providers', 6 June 2025 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the money-laundering-risk quote and FSM Act s.136(1)/s.137 citations for the DTSP regime

MAS: Notice PSN02 -- Prevention of Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism -- Digital Payment Token Service (landing page) -- link only; every www.mas.gov.sg path tried this session, across multiple dated PDF versions, served an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page to this project's honest User-Agent, plain curl and WebFetch alike, verified today; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate

MAS: Guidelines to Notice PSN02 on Prevention of Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism -- Digital Payment Token Service -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg blocked on this path this session

MAS: enforcement action, 'MAS Reprimands Three Arrows Capital for Providing False Information and Exceeding Assets Under Management Threshold' (30 Jun 2022) -- link only, www.mas.gov.sg blocked on direct fetch this session; per available search-indexed summary this is an RFMC registration/false-information matter, not an AML/CFT-specific action -- listed for reference and to document this research's negative finding, not cited as AML/CFT enforcement precedent

MAS: enforcement action, 'MAS Takes Regulatory Actions against 9 Financial Institutions for AML-Related Breaches' (Jul 2025) -- link only, www.mas.gov.sg blocked on direct fetch this session; concerns traditional banks/capital-markets-services licensees/a trust company tied to the Aug 2023 S$3bn money-laundering case, not digital-payment-token or digital-token-service-provider business -- listed to document this research's search for, and non-finding of, DPT/DTSP-specific AML enforcement

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg): Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 -- link only; returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried this session, including robots.txt; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate, listed for reference only

open_items: MAS Notice PSN02 -- the core operative AML/CFT rulebook for DPT service providers -- and its accompanying Guidelines could not be directly fetched this session: every path tried against www.mas.gov.sg, across the 5 December 2019, 28 June 2021, 1 March 2022, 2 April 2024, and 30 June 2025 dated PDF versions, returned an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML bot-block page. This card's account of PSN02's version lineage and its content (risk assessment, CDD, travel rule, record-keeping, STR-to-STRO reporting, internal AML/CFT policy/compliance/audit/training requirements) rests on search-engine-indexed document titles and secondary compliance-industry summaries (e.g. ComplyAdvantage, Merkle Science, Waystone), not on independently read primary text. This should be re-fetched and quoted directly once mas.gov.sg is reachable, or via an alternate genuinely primary mirror if one is found (no sgpc.gov.sg mirror of a PSN02 PDF specifically was located this session).

The Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 Part 9 DTSP regime's own dedicated AML/CFT Notice (reported in secondary trade sources as 'MAS Notice FSM-N27') and the Financial Services and Markets (Digital Token Service Providers) Regulations 2025 -- both reported effective 30 June 2025 -- were not independently fetched this session; both are hosted only on the blocked www.mas.gov.sg domain. Their reported content (CDD on all existing customers, an independent AML/CFT audit function, 5-working-day suspicious-activity/fraud-incident reporting) is stated from secondary legal-commentary sources only and should be independently re-verified once mas.gov.sg is reachable.

This research specifically investigated the director's brief's suggestion of the Three Arrows Capital (30 June 2022) reprimand as an AML/CFT enforcement example and found, from available search-indexed summaries of MAS's own enforcement-action page (itself blocked to direct fetch this session), that the reprimand concerned false/misleading information to MAS and breach of a registered-fund-management-company assets-under-management ceiling -- a fund-management registration and disclosure matter, not an AML/CFT-specific finding. This card does not cite it as AML/CFT enforcement precedent. It should be independently re-verified against MAS's own enforcement-action page directly once mas.gov.sg is reachable, in case the underlying (unfetched) enforcement notice contains AML/CFT findings not reflected in secondary summaries.

This research searched specifically for a MAS enforcement action grounded in PSN02 or otherwise targeting a named DPT service provider or FSM Act Part 9 DTSP licensee for an AML/CFT breach, and found none as of this card's generation date. Two 2025 AML/CFT-related MAS enforcement actions were found and are recorded here only as negative/background evidence, not as digital-asset precedent: composition penalties totalling S$960,000 against five major payment institutions (27 June 2025) for breaches of the separate Notice PSN01 (specified payment services / cross-border money transfer, not DPT service) between March 2020 and August 2023; and composition penalties, a licence revocation, and prohibition orders totalling S$27.45 million against nine financial institutions, a capital-markets-services licensee, a licensed trust company, and several named individuals (announced July 2025), all tied to the August 2023 S$3 billion money-laundering case and none of them a DPT service provider or DTSP licensee. Should MAS take AML/CFT enforcement action against a named digital-asset business, this pillar should be updated per the named-entity rule.

This card's statement that suspicious transaction reports from DPT/DTSP licensees are filed with the Suspicious Transaction Reporting Office (STRO), a unit of the Commercial Affairs Department (CAD) of the Singapore Police Force, is drawn from secondary compliance-industry descriptions of PSN02's own 'STRO' definition, not from this research's own direct reading of PSN02's text (blocked, see above). police.gov.sg / CAD is not itself a registered citation domain in config/jurisdictions/sg.json under this project's registry-first convention, so no CAD-hosted source is cited directly; this should be revisited (including whether to register a CAD/police.gov.sg citation entry) once PSN02's own text can be independently fetched and its STRO definition read directly.

The Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021's exact enacted citation and its 'Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 (Commencement) Notification 2024' gazettal details (reported in secondary sources as gazetted around 28 March-2 April 2024) could not be independently confirmed against Singapore Statutes Online, which returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried this session, including robots.txt. This card's account of the Amendment Act's FATF-alignment purpose and First Schedule mechanism rests on direct fetch of the enabling Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg), which is Bill text rather than the enacted, consolidated statute; its 4 April 2024 actual commencement date is corroborated independently by MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release. Should be re-verified against the consolidated Act text directly once sso.agc.gov.sg is reachable.

MAS's own consultation history recited in its 6 June 2025 media release -- a first response to public consultation (14 February 2022), the Consultation Paper on Proposed Regulatory Approach, Regulations, Notices and Guidelines for DTSPs (4 October 2024), and MAS's Response to Feedback Received (30 May 2025) -- was not independently fetched this session; all three documents link only to the blocked www.mas.gov.sg domain. Their existence and dates are stated only as recited inside the genuinely fetched 6 June 2025 release, not as independently confirmed primary documents in their own right.

This pillar covers MAS's AML/CFT regulatory and enforcement framework for digital-asset business (PSN02, the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021's FATF-alignment mechanism, and the FSM Act Part 9 DTSP regime's AML/CFT rationale) only. The FSM Act Part 9 DTSP regime's licensing/scope mechanics as they bear on dealing, custody and advisory activity are tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar and are not restated here beyond the AML/CFT-specific quote above, to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.
